Islamic Tradition

Averroes (Ibn Rushd)

1126 – 1198

The Commentator: a rigorous Aristotelianism that provokes centuries of European debate on the intellect.

To extract a philosophy of mind from Averroes, the first correction is methodological. His psychology cannot be cleanly detached from his metaphysics, cosmology, logic, and religious writings. The current Stanford Encyclopedia entry on Ibn Rushd stresses the permeability, in his corpus, of metaphysics, natural philosophy, psychology, and theology, and an Arabic-language study by Abdelali Elamrani-Jamal similarly treats conception (taṣawwur) and assent (taṣdīq) as threads that bind logic, psychology, and metaphysics into one rational architecture. That matters because Averroes’s account of human thought is inseparable from his account of celestial intellects and divine knowledge.

It is also a mistake to treat God, prophecy, or “angelic” minds as theological leftovers that can be bracketed off. On Averroes’s own terms, human intellection sits at the lowest edge of a wider hierarchy of intellectual beings; celestial beings possess souls, intellect, and desire; and the First Cause is the ultimate intelligible that orders the whole noetic cosmos. So if one wants a comprehensive Averroist philosophy of mind, supra-human minds are not appendices to the story. They are part of its structure.

There is also a philological warning. The relevant materials are dispersed across the short, middle, and long commentaries on De anima, the epitomes on the Parva naturalia, the Incoherence of the Incoherence, the Kashf, the Decisive Treatise, and the conjunction treatises. Some key works survive only in medieval Hebrew or Latin translations; in particular, the mature Long Commentary on De anima is extant complete in Latin and only fragmentarily in Arabic. That is one reason not to flatten “Averroes’s doctrine” into a single timeless position: his account of intellect develops substantially across the corpus.

The soul as form, power, and natural principle

At the most basic level, Averroes remains rigorously Aristotelian. Soul is the substantial form of a living body. He distinguishes nutritive, sensory, and rational levels, but he is flexible about whether these are literally different “souls” or powers and principles within one soul. What matters more to him is whether a given power differs from another merely by function or also by substrate. In other words, his psychology is organized less by a fixed census of faculties than by an analysis of what operations require what kinds of subjects.

Yet this hylomorphic starting point does not make his account merely terrestrial. The Arabic-Islamic psychology entry in SEP emphasizes that Averroes presents the soul of living beings as produced through natural causes that are both proximate and remote, earthly and celestial. That point is easy to underestimate, but it matters a great deal: from the start, his psychology is cosmological. Mind is not an accidental late arrival in a dead universe; it arises within an ordered world whose lower forms depend upon higher motions and higher intelligences.

Embodied cognition: sensation, common sense, imagination, cogitation, memory

Averroes’s account of cognition begins with receptivity. The “foundation” of cognition, as the SEP entry puts it, is passivity. The external senses receive the sensible qualities of bodies, and the common sense (al-ḥiss al-mushtarak) integrates them into a unified perceptual field. Averroes treats common sense as the point at which perception becomes both multisensory and, in a minimal but important way, self-aware. He still repeats the traditional claim that common sense originates in the heart, yet he also says its operation terminates in the brain, where the higher sensory powers take over.

Those higher powers are imagination (al-mutakhayyila), the cogitative or discriminative power (al-mufakkira, al-mumayyiza), and memory (al-ḥāfiẓa, al-dhākira). Averroes locates them in the brain and gives the cogitative faculty a decisive role. Instead of following Avicenna in positing a separate estimative faculty, he makes the cogitative power do the bodily, individual work of discriminating the “intentions” embedded in images. Spanish scholarship, especially Josep Puig Montada, usefully underlines that this faculty is bodily and particular, not itself the intellect.

He is also more physiological than many readers expect. The Arabic SEP article notes that good memory depends on dryness in parts of the brain and poor memory on moisture. It also explains that the cogitative faculty can be identified, in one register, with the practical intellect, because it prepares sensory and imaginative content for choice and deliberation before the theoretical intellect abstracts universals from that prepared material. So Averroes’s philosophy of mind includes a serious bodily psychology of attention, retention, discrimination, and action-preparation.

What moves through this system are not yet universals, but increasingly refined representations. Sensory forms become images; the cogitative faculty extracts “individual contents” or intentions (maʿānī, intentiones); memory stores them; and the whole process is compared to stripping rinds until the fruit appears. Nonhuman animals, lacking the distinctively human cogitative power, do not pass beyond superficial sensory appearances in the same way. This is why Averroes’s psychology is strongly empiricist without being reductively sensory: the route to thought always begins in sense, but it does not end there.

The intellects: agent, material, and the stages of understanding

Universal thought begins where bodily powers stop being sufficient. In the mature doctrine two intellects are decisive. The Agent Intellect (ʿaql al-faʿʿāl) actualizes or “illuminates” imagined intentions, while the Material or Hylic Intellect (ʿaql al-hayūlānī) receives intelligibles in act. “Material” here does not mean bodily stuff. It means a matter-like receptivity in the intelligible order. Averroes also preserves stages such as intellect in habitu and the acquired intellect to describe the individual’s achieved disposition of knowing.

But even in the Long Commentary, the two separate intellects are parts of the soul only equivocally. They are not the actuality of a body in the normal hylomorphic sense. Taylor puts the point starkly: the rational side is not properly soul as form of the body, but something shared through the presence of the agent and material intellect during earthly life. This is one of the deepest reasons Averroes is so difficult for later Christian and modern readers alike: the most universal part of our thought is ours by participation, not by simple intrinsic possession.

The developmental arc across the three De anima commentaries

Any adequate report has to keep the development in view. Taylor’s reconstruction remains especially helpful here. In the Short Commentary, each human being has a personal material intellect understood as a disposition of the forms in imagination. In the Middle Commentary, the material intellect becomes immaterial and separate, yet still belongs to an individual in a qualified sense. In the Long Commentary, both agent and material intellect are shared transcendent substances, with the material intellect now one in number for all humans. The agent intellect is shared throughout, but the receptive side of thinking changes dramatically.

The Long Commentary therefore introduces one of the strangest ontologies in medieval philosophy. The material intellect is described in later scholarship as a “fourth kind of being” and as the last of the separate intellects in the celestial hierarchy. It is not ordinary matter, not a bodily form, and not a normal composite. The point of the move is to secure the incorruptibility and objectivity of intelligibles. But the price is obvious: human intellection becomes partly supra-individual.

Why Averroes arrives at the unity of intellect

Why did Averroes adopt the notorious unity thesis? The recent SEP article reconstructs the pressure elegantly. He wants to preserve three claims at once: human beings do think abstractly; abstract thought cannot belong to a corporeal organ; and human beings are bodily animals. The shared-material-intellect doctrine is his way of keeping all three. The body supplies images and intentions; the separate intellect supplies universality.

There is also a second, less metaphysical and more epistemic pressure: intersubjective science. Taylor shows that Averroes comes to think that if intelligibles in act were multiplied in separate private intellects, common science and genuine intellectual discourse would become impossible. The SEP entry makes the same point very directly: without literally shared concepts, teachers could not communicate knowledge to students. So the unity thesis is not simply a mystical flourish. It is meant to ground the objectivity and communicability of science.

How can “this human” still think?

This generates the classic objection: if intellect is shared, how can this individual understand? Averroes’s answer is that “the soul never thinks without an image.” The separate material intellect depends for its very operation on imagined intentions supplied by individuals, and each person partly governs thought because each person governs the imagistic and cogitative preparations that trigger intellection. The material intellect is eternal only at the level of the species; the actual exercise of thought is activated through individual phantasms.

Taylor’s formula that the Agent Intellect is “form for us” captures the same strategy. The agent intellect is not external to thought in the way a remote craftsman is external to a finished product. It is present in the individual’s act of understanding through will, preparation, abstraction, and reception. The separate intellects are transcendent, but they are not functionally detached from human cognitive life.

One modern analogy, proposed in SEP, is to an extended mind: human cognition extends beyond the individual organism. That analogy is useful, but only with caution. Averroes’s view is much more hierarchical and metaphysical than contemporary extended-mind theory. Still, the comparison does bring out something real: on his mature view, the thinking subject is not bounded by the body alone.

What is personal, and what is impersonal, in the human mind?

This yields a deeply split account of personhood. What is most personal in mental life is not the universal intelligible but the embodied side of cognition: sensation, common sense, imagination, cogitation, memory, and the voluntary preparation for thought. What is least personal is precisely what makes science universally true: the intelligible in act and the material intellect that receives it. Puig Montada is especially clear that conjunction with the material intellect is never direct; it is always mediated by imagined forms and above all by the cogitative faculty. Italian reference works summarize the result succinctly: universality and truth are preserved, but individuality is attenuated.

A reasonable philosophical inference is that Averroes’s best candidate for lived, first-person consciousness is not the separate intellect but the embodied nexus of common sense, imagination, memory, and cogitation. Common sense is where multisensory unity and perceptual self-awareness occur; memory and cogitation preserve the individual’s history and practical orientation. The separate intellect yields universality, but the phenomenology of being this person seems to remain lower down, in the internal senses.

That is one reason the Latin objection “how can this man understand?” bites so hard. Averroes tries to preserve both universal science and individual thinking, but the price is a hybrid subject: not a private mind with fully intrinsic concepts, yet not a mere channel through which a wholly alien intellect passes. His view is strongest precisely where it refuses those two extremes.

Conjunction, felicity, and immortality

Averroes’s philosophy of mind is teleological. The soul is not fully explained until one states its perfection. The Arabic SEP entry says that the striving of theoretical intellect for universal truth is the soul’s highest and “very divine” activity, and that conjunction (ittiṣāl) with the Agent Intellect is the culminating unification of knower and known. The Ibn Rushd SEP entry reports that at this peak the human being takes on “eternal existence,” is “made like unto God,” and in some sense becomes one of the incorporeal beings. In other words, the true telos of mind is contemplative assimilation to higher intellect.

But conjunction does not straightforwardly amount to personal immortality. SEP and Taylor both read the mature philosophical doctrine as giving no rational basis for the continued existence of the individual soul after bodily death; if anything remains, it would be one in number rather than many. Taylor is especially forceful that neither the Short nor Middle Commentary provides a rational basis for post-mortem personal survival, and the Long Commentary does not restore one.

A valuable Spanish interpretation by Abel Miró i Comas formulates the outcome by saying that the soul is “mortal and immortal” in different respects: mortal insofar as the individual imaginative-cogitative complex belongs to this particular person and perishes, immortal insofar as conjunction touches what is eternal and divine. That is a useful interpretive shorthand rather than an uncontested formula of Averroes himself, but it catches a genuine tension. Italian summaries reach a similar conclusion by speaking of an impersonal immortality.

Divine and celestial minds

I will use the phrase “angelic minds” cautiously, because Averroes’s philosophical vocabulary is mainly that of celestial souls and separate intelligences. But the substance is clear. Celestial beings have souls, intellect, and desire; because they lack sense perception, their desire is united with intellect and ordered toward divine perfection; and their motions sustain the cosmic order below the moon. So the human mind, for Averroes, belongs within a graded continuum of supra-human minds rather than standing alone in nature.

This hierarchy is not ornamental cosmology. The Arabic SEP article says that the material intellect in the Long Commentary is the last of the separate intellects, and Taylor notes that in the Middle Commentary the relation of a separate intellect to the human being is modeled partly on the way a celestial soul is “in” its celestial body without ordinary hylomorphic composition. So the relation between human and higher intellect is not accidental. It is structurally analogous to the very way the cosmos itself is ordered.

The divine mind stands at the summit of this order, but it is not a larger version of the human mind. God, Averroes says, thinks nothing outside himself; divine self-thinking is identical with thinking all existence; and God knows the created world neither universally nor particularly in the human sense, because divine knowledge is knowledge as cause, not knowledge caused by things. In his philosophical cosmology, God functions chiefly as final and formal cause for the celestial order, and divine providence extends over all existing things without becoming a special providence for each individual in the way many theologians demanded.

Recent scholarship has complicated the picture in useful ways. Jean-Baptiste Brenet argues that the neglected concept of relation is central to Averroes’s treatment of divine knowledge of particulars, while Yehuda Halper argues that the Epistle on Divine Knowledge is best read as a dialectical inquiry or philosophical training text rather than as a simple statement of final metaphysical doctrine. So on the divine side of Averroes’s philosophy of mind, the broad structure is clear, but the argumentative form is still actively debated.

Prophecy and revelation are part of the noetic picture

Prophecy should not be treated as a distraction from psychology. In the Kashf, prophecy is foundational to religion; miracles are only external signs; and the stronger proof of prophetic veracity is the Qur’an itself and the law’s capacity to promote human happiness. In the Decisive Treatise, Averroes insists that demonstrative reasoning cannot truly conflict with revelation: “truth does not oppose truth.” So revelation, law, rhetoric, and demonstration are different registers within one ordered account of how human beings are guided toward truth and flourishing.

At the same time, one should not force Averroes into a Farabian or Avicennian model in which prophecy is fully absorbed into a single naturalized theory of imaginative cognition. Taylor argues that the mature Long Commentary on De anima contains no explicit philosophical integration of prophecy into natural human psychology, even though Averroes’s religious writings discuss prophecy at length. That absence is revealing: his philosophy of mind is distributed across genres, with demonstrative psychology doing one job and religious-philosophical writings another.

The most defensible philosophy of mind one can extract from Averroes

What emerges, in compressed form, is this.
1. Human mindedness is hylomorphically embodied at its base. All cognition begins with sensory passivity, common sense, and the internal senses.
2. Mental content is representationally staged. Images and intentions are progressively refined before universals appear.
3. Universal intellection is not reducible to a bodily organ. It requires agent and material intellect irreducible to corporeal powers.
4. Science is intrinsically shared. Averroes rejects a purely private concept-space because intelligibles must be literally common if teaching and intersubjective knowledge are to be possible.
5. Personal subjectivity is hybrid. What is most “mine” is the sensory-imaginal-cogitative-memorial side of thought; what is most universally true in thought is shared and supra-individual.
6. Mind is teleological. Its perfection is contemplative conjunction with the Agent Intellect and the attainment of a quasi-divine happiness.
7. Supra-human minds are intrinsic to psychology. Celestial intellects and the divine intellect are higher strata of the same noetic order, not external theological add-ons.
8. Revelation and philosophy are coordinated, not simply rival, modes within a larger theory of human guidance to truth.

A note on what the non-Anglophone material changes

The non-Anglophone material genuinely improves the picture. Spanish work by Josep Puig Montada sharpens the bodily status of cogitation and the indirectness of conjunction through imagined forms. Abel Miró i Comas pushes the mortality/immortality problem into a more dialectical formulation. Italian syntheses in Treccani crisply state the trade-off between universal truth and impersonal immortality. The Arabic study by Elamrani-Jamal makes what may be the most important methodological correction of all: it resists isolating the unity-of-intellect thesis from the larger unity of logic, psychology, and metaphysics. That last move is especially valuable because it helps recover Averroes from the narrow frame of the Latin condemnations.

My own judgment, after laying these sources side by side, is that the most faithful “Averroist philosophy of mind” is a hierarchical and distributed noetics. It is embodied without being materialist, supra-individual without abolishing individual experience, and theological in a way that is internal rather than externally appended. The deepest tension in it is also its deepest insight: what is most universal in thought is least private, and what is most private in us is not intellect in its fullest sense. That tension is exactly why Averroes remained philosophically explosive.

For research purposes, the indispensable set of texts to hold together are the three De anima commentaries, the Parva naturalia epitomes, the conjunction treatises, the Incoherence, the Kashf, and the Decisive Treatise. Only across that whole set do human mind, celestial intellect, prophecy, and divine knowledge come properly into one view.
